topic: Someone finds their college application essay published as an example
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy paper felt cold beneath my fingertips, a stark contrast to the warmth of the coffee mug I still clutched. I’d found it tucked between a stack of discarded brochures in the university bookstore—my own essay, perfectly typeset, highlighted in a tasteful blue font, sitting next to a note: "Sample Submission: Insightful Narrative." My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake that stole the air from my lungs. I traced the words with a shaky finger, the carefully crafted vulnerability I’d poured onto that page suddenly feeling exposed, laid bare for public consumption. A dull ache settled low in my stomach, tightening my chest until it felt like breathing was a conscious, difficult effort. I slowly folded the paper, the sound of the rustling sound amplifying the silence of the deserted aisle.

[story 2]

My hands were shaking so badly I had to grip the edge of the desk to keep them steady. The screen glowed, illuminating the words I had painstakingly labored over for weeks. It was my personal narrative, the one I thought was uniquely mine, now displayed on a public-facing portal, labeled clearly as an exemplary piece. A wave of nausea washed over me, making the room tilt slightly. I leaned back, staring blankly at the screen, my jaw clenched so tightly my molars ached. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and brittle. The realization hit me with physical force: this intimate piece of self was now fodder for others to dissect. I pushed the chair back with a screech, the noise echoing in the quiet room, and walked toward the window, watching the indifferent city lights blur into streaks of meaningless color.

[story 3]

I sat on the edge of my bed, the essay spread out on my lap like a damning piece of evidence. Every carefully chosen adjective, every hesitant pause, was now cataloged, analyzed by an unseen audience. My gaze kept flicking back and forth between the text and my own reflection in the darkened television screen—a pale, strained face etched with confusion and a deep sense of betrayal. I pressed the heels of my palms against my eyes, trying to physically scrub the image away. A hot flush crept up my neck, staining my cheeks crimson. I picked up a pen, intending to cross out a sentence, but the ink seemed to mock my efforts, refusing to smear the perceived damage. The silence in the room was heavy, weighted down by the sheer magnitude of what had just been revealed.
